identified_error: The paper treated 65 procedures as independent observations even though they came from only 61 patients, with many bilateral cases (simultaneous or staged fasciotomies). In the regression and other statistical analyses, each operated leg/procedure was analyzed as if unrelated to the others, despite outcomes and baseline characteristics from two legs in the same patient being correlated and in some cases patient-level variables being duplicated across both limbs. This is a unit-of-analysis / nonindependence error (pseudoreplication).
why_fatal: The main conclusions of the paper depend on multivariable regression identifying 'independent predictors' such as male sex, younger age, depression, and deep posterior compartment involvement. Those analyses require independent observations. When bilateral limbs from the same patient are counted separately without accounting for clustering within patient, standard errors are underestimated, confidence intervals are too narrow, and P values can become spuriously significant. Because the sample was already small and the reported significant predictors were marginal, this analytical error can easily create false-positive associations. Thus the claim that these variables independently predict better outcomes is not statistically valid.
correct_approach: The analysis should have used the patient, not the procedure/leg, as the primary unit of analysis, or else used a method that accounts for within-patient correlation when both limbs were included (eg, mixed-effects models, generalized estimating equations, or cluster-robust standard errors with patient as the clustering variable). If outcomes were only available at the patient level, bilateral procedures should not have been entered as separate observations at all. Given the very small number of depressed patients and the limited sample size, the multivariable model also should have been simplified or avoided to prevent overfitting.
additional_errors: Severe risk of overfitting: only about 61 patients but 6 predictors in the regression, with just 5 patients having depression, making coefficient estimates unstable.
Response rate was only 58%, creating substantial potential for nonresponse bias.
Use of change scores as dependent variables can be problematic, especially without careful adjustment for baseline values and regression to the mean.
The manuscript reports 'Mann-Whitney U tests' to compare pre- and postoperative scores in the same cohort; a paired test would have been more appropriate.
Case-series design without a control group cannot support strong causal claims that fasciotomy is effective relative to alternatives.
